stories.headline: Denver City Council unanimously bans masks for ICE agents and other law enforcement operating in city
stories.summary:
Denver City Council voted unanimously Monday to ban federal immigration officers and other law enforcement from wearing masks while working within city limits. The ordinance also requires federal agents to visibly identify themselves with a badge or ID from at least 25 feet away during operations in Denver. Officers who do not comply could be cited or arrested by Denver police, though exemptions apply to undercover operations, SWAT, tactical, and emergency responses.

Council member Flor Alvidrez, a co-sponsor of the measure, said constituents had expressed fear about being unable to distinguish masked, armed individuals from bad actors. The ordinance comes days after Mayor Mike Johnston signed an executive order banning ICE from city-owned property and requiring body cameras during enforcement actions, which Johnston described as a combined strategy to protect residents from federal overreach.

Denver Police Chief Ron Thomas acknowledged that enforcement details remain unresolved, saying officers will use their own discretion and the city needs to 'move forward carefully.' Johnston had not yet signed the ordinance as of Monday evening, and the effective date was not immediately known.

Denver joins Los Angeles, New York, Chicago, Boston, Oakland, and Seattle in passing measures aimed at restricting aggressive immigration enforcement tactics, following a high-profile ICE crackdown in Minneapolis earlier this winter.

stories.headline: Colorado legislature advances gun control bills targeting 3-D printed firearms and gun barrel sales
stories.summary:
Colorado lawmakers advanced two gun control measures Monday, with the state House voting 40-25 to pass a bill banning 3-D printed firearms and the Senate voting 19-16 to advance a bill requiring gun barrels to be sold only through federally licensed dealers. Both bills now move to the opposite chamber, and both are expected to advance to Gov. Jared Polis. All votes were along party lines, with Democrats exclusively backing the measures.

House Bill 1144 would prohibit manufacturing 3-D printed firearms and components, including large-capacity magazines and unfinished frames, and bans distribution of instruction manuals for producing such devices. First-time violations would be a misdemeanor; subsequent violations would be a felony. Senate Bill 43 would require in-person sales of gun barrels through licensed dealers and mandate five-year record retention.

Sponsors framed both bills as efforts to crack down on ghost guns — untraceable firearms without serial numbers. Colorado Bureau of Investigation data shows 89 ghost guns seized between August 2024 and December 2025. Sen. Tom Sullivan, co-sponsoring both bills, is the father of a victim of the 2012 Aurora theater shooting.

Republicans argued both measures are unconstitutional and infringe on Second Amendment rights, characterizing them as attacks on law-abiding gun owners rather than effective crime-fighting tools.

stories.headline: Denver submits bid to host 2028 Democratic National Convention
stories.summary:
Mayor Mike Johnston announced Monday that Denver is one of five cities under consideration to host the 2028 Democratic National Convention. The other contenders are Atlanta, Boston, Chicago, and Philadelphia. Denver last hosted the convention in 2008.

Johnston's announcement positions Denver as a high-profile candidate at a time when the city has been at the center of national debates over immigration enforcement and federal-local tensions. No timeline for a decision by Democratic National Committee officials was immediately provided.

Hosting a major national convention would generate significant economic activity for the city, including hotel bookings, catering, transportation, and security contracts. The 2008 DNC at Invesco Field at Mile High drew an estimated 50,000 delegates, journalists, and guests to Denver.

stories.headline: Colorado lawmakers advance bill strengthening protections for sexual assault and domestic violence survivors
stories.summary:
A Colorado Senate committee unanimously approved Senate Bill 26-095 on Monday, sending it to the full Senate for debate. The legislation would allow victims of sexual assault, domestic violence, stalking, and sex trafficking to testify via closed-circuit television from outside the courtroom, and would mandate annual two-hour training for law enforcement on trauma-informed responses to victims.

State Sen. Mike Weissman, D-Aurora, a co-sponsor and chairman of the Senate Judiciary Committee, called the bill one 'about survivorship.' Colorado Attorney General Phil Weiser also testified in support, saying 'our criminal justice system needs to do better.' A state public defender spokesperson cautioned that the closed-circuit testimony provision could face constitutional challenges under the Sixth Amendment right to confront accusers.

The hearing featured testimony from more than a dozen advocates and survivors, including a woman who said police asked her if she was on drugs after she suffered a panic attack while reporting domestic violence. Another witness, Kelly Tobin, described reporting a rape by her medical driver in 2024 and having the case declined by the Denver DA's office.

The bill follows a Denver Gazette investigation finding that for every 10 rape reports in Colorado over the past decade, only one arrest resulted — a rate roughly three times lower than for other serious crimes against persons in the state.

stories.headline: Update: Broomfield High School excludes 26 unvaccinated students; Colorado confirms third measles case of 2026
stories.summary:
Twenty-six students at Broomfield High School were barred from attending class Monday following confirmation of a measles case at the school last week. The number excluded had dropped from 42 on Friday as families submitted proof of vaccination. School officials said academic support plans for excluded students would be communicated by mid-week. The high school enrolls approximately 1,671 students.

Colorado health officials separately confirmed a third measles case in the state, an unvaccinated elementary school-aged child from Arapahoe County who tested positive in February. The Broomfield student had no known out-of-state travel or identified exposure source, prompting state officials to warn that 'unidentified measles cases may be occurring in or traveling through the area.'

Colorado's measles vaccination rate among students stands at 93.3%, below the 95% threshold needed for community protection. The state recorded 36 measles cases in 2025 — more than the previous decade combined — with about one in four linked to an out-of-state traveler who flew while infectious. Nationally, case counts have risen sharply in 2026.

Measles was considered eradicated in the United States in 2000. Before a vaccine was developed, an estimated 48,000 Americans were hospitalized annually and 500 died each year from the disease.

stories.headline: Avalanche beat Kings 4-2 in Los Angeles, extend Central Division lead as Toews scores late winner
stories.summary:
Devon Toews scored the go-ahead goal late in the third period and Martin Necas added an empty-net goal and two assists as the Colorado Avalanche defeated the Los Angeles Kings 4-2 Monday night in Los Angeles. The win came in the Kings' first game under a new head coach.

Colorado now sits atop the overall NHL standings with a 39-10-9 record. The victory was particularly notable given the Avalanche's recent challenges in maintaining their Central Division lead, which had previously shrunk to five points as the Minnesota Wild and Dallas Stars surged.

Los Angeles, at 24-21-14, is fighting to hold its playoff positioning in the Western Conference. The Kings' managerial transition adds further uncertainty to their playoff push as they faced one of the league's top teams in a pivotal stretch.

stories.headline: Nuggets sign veteran point guard Tyus Jones to fill 15th roster spot for playoff push
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets signed point guard Tyus Jones to a minimum contract for the remainder of the season Monday, filling the team's last open roster spot. Jones, 29, was waived by the Dallas Mavericks on Saturday after being part of a three-team trade at the February deadline that sent Anthony Davis to Washington. He previously played for Orlando and Charlotte this season.

The Nuggets sought ball-handling depth, outside shooting, and veteran experience after failing to land preferred buyout targets Kyle Anderson, who re-signed with Minnesota, and Khris Middleton, who stayed in Dallas. Jones carries a career assist-to-turnover ratio of 5.5 and holds the single-season record of 7.29 (minimum 100 assists) set in 2023-24 with Washington.

Jones joins Jalen Pickett and Bruce Brown as reserve point guard options behind Jamal Murray. Denver has struggled at times to bring the ball up against full-court pressure. The signing comes as the Nuggets fell to fifth place in the Western Conference after a Sunday loss to Minnesota — their lowest standing since early November.

Jones is averaging career lows in points (3.1), field goal percentage (34.9%), and three-point percentage (28.1%) this season. His defensive limitations have been a consistent weakness throughout his career, but the Nuggets are banking on his ball-handling steadiness to bolster their playoff rotation.

stories.headline: Jamal Murray erupts for 45 points as Nuggets beat Jazz in back-to-back victory
stories.summary:
Jamal Murray scored 45 points Monday night as the Denver Nuggets defeated the Utah Jazz in a back-to-back game, providing a needed win after Sunday's loss to Minnesota dropped Denver to fifth in the Western Conference. Murray's performance came one night after he posted 25 points in a 117-108 defeat to the Timberwolves.

The Jazz entered the game ranked last in the NBA in points allowed, giving up 125.7 per game, making them a favorable matchup for an offense-focused Nuggets squad. Murray is averaging 25.3 points, 7.3 assists, and 4.4 rebounds per game this season.

The win provides Denver some momentum as the team attempts to build its first winning streak since January, with the Nuggets now aiming to improve their playoff seeding in a competitive Western Conference race.

stories.headline: One man killed in two-vehicle crash on Belleview Avenue in Cherry Hills Village
stories.summary:
An adult man died Monday after a two-vehicle crash at 11:52 a.m. on Belleview Avenue near University Boulevard in Cherry Hills Village, according to police Chief Jason Lyons. No other injuries were reported. The crash prompted Cherry Hills Village police to close westbound lanes of Belleview Avenue for approximately four hours before reopening them around 4 p.m.

Lyons did not immediately identify the victim or provide details on the circumstances of the collision. The crash is under investigation by Cherry Hills Village police. Belleview Avenue at University Boulevard is a heavily trafficked corridor connecting the south Denver suburbs.

stories.headline: Total lunar eclipse visible over Denver early Tuesday morning — last until 2029
stories.summary:
A total lunar eclipse will be visible over Denver in the predawn hours of Tuesday, March 3, with the partial eclipse beginning at 2:50 a.m. and the total eclipse commencing at 4:04 a.m. Maximum darkness occurs at 4:33 a.m., with the total eclipse phase ending at 5:02 a.m. The moon sets at 6:33 a.m., four minutes before sunrise.

Denver will not have another total lunar eclipse until June 2029, making Tuesday's event notable for skywatchers. A partial lunar eclipse will also be visible from Denver on Aug. 28 of this year. The city's most recent total lunar eclipses occurred in May 2021, May 2022, November 2022, and March 2025.

A lunar eclipse occurs when the Earth passes between a full moon and the sun, casting its shadow across the lunar surface. Two super moons are also expected this year on Nov. 24 and Dec. 23.

stories.headline: EchoStar posts $14.5 billion loss after selling wireless assets and halting 5G network
stories.summary:
Douglas County-based EchoStar Corp. reported a $14.5 billion net loss for 2025 after recording $17.63 billion in non-cash charges tied to its decision to halt its 5G network business following Federal Communications Commission scrutiny. The charges were connected to the company's sale of its wireless assets.

The staggering loss marks a dramatic financial reckoning for one of Colorado's largest technology companies. EchoStar has been restructuring significantly over the past two years, shedding core assets as it navigated regulatory pressure and strategic shifts in its wireless ambitions.

The company's results underscore the turbulent trajectory of its attempt to build a nationwide 5G network, an effort that drew sustained regulatory attention before the company ultimately abandoned the initiative.

stories.headline: California firm acquires 112,500-square-foot Centennial manufacturing facility for $21.5 million
stories.summary:
The Koll Co., a California-based real estate investment firm, purchased a 112,500-square-foot manufacturing facility in Centennial for $21.5 million, marking the company's latest industrial investment in the Denver metro region. The deal was recorded Monday.

The acquisition reflects continued out-of-state investor interest in Denver-area industrial real estate, a sector that has seen elevated demand driven by e-commerce, logistics, and manufacturing activity along the Front Range. Centennial, located in Arapahoe County south of Denver, is home to a mix of light industrial, office, and retail development.

Details on the facility's current tenants or intended use under new ownership were not immediately available.

weather.notable: Nearly 5 degrees below the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: Parenting is basically just saying 'Put your shoes on' 47 times before you leave the house.

topStory.headline: US-Israeli Strike Kills Iran's Supreme Leader, Triggering Regional Missile Attacks and Global Economic Shock
topStory.summary:
A joint US-Israeli aerial bombardment of Iran over the weekend killed Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei along with a large portion of Iran's military and political leadership, triggering an immediate and sweeping Iranian retaliatory response. Iran launched missiles and drones at Israeli and American military installations across the Gulf, striking bases in Qatar, Kuwait, Jordan, the United Arab Emirates and Bahrain, as well as targeting the US embassy in Riyadh and civilian infrastructure including hotels. The Trump administration, through Secretary of State Marco Rubio, described the pre-emptive US strikes as a response to Israel's determination to attack Iran and the certainty that American troops would be targeted.

Gulf states joined the United States in issuing a joint statement strongly condemning what they called Iran's "indiscriminate and reckless missile and drone attacks," warning the strikes threatened broader regional stability. More than 100,000 British nationals were stranded across the Gulf region as of Monday, with airspace closed to most commercial flights and overland evacuation routes considered too dangerous. The rapidly escalating conflict has drawn in countries across the Middle East and raised fears of a wider regional war that governments and international observers had long sought to prevent.

Global energy markets reacted sharply, with Brent crude oil rising approximately 8.5% on Monday to around $79 a barrel, continuing a climb from just above $60 in January as US-Iran tensions had been building throughout the year. European benchmark natural gas prices surged 38% after state-owned QatarEnergy announced it was halting production at two sites following drone strikes on its facilities. Shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, which carries roughly 20% of global oil supplies, was disrupted as tankers declined to transit the waterway and insurers pulled back from offering coverage.

Economists at Goldman Sachs warned that a worst-case scenario involving a complete one-month blockage of the Strait of Hormuz could push oil prices up by as much as an additional $15 per barrel. The price spike arrives at a difficult moment for central banks in Europe and the United Kingdom, which had only recently brought inflation from post-pandemic and post-Ukraine war highs under control. In the UK, the probability of a Bank of England interest rate cut at its March 19 meeting fell from roughly 80% to 69% on Monday morning, reflecting market concern that sustained energy cost increases could reignite inflationary pressure.
